scenario_results.reason: The lane progressed complete recurring bookkeeping records into posting, matching, reconciliation, and close-prep status with named source evidence.
Missing receipts, invoices, remittance details, and tenant coding rules blocked affected items and produced a close-prep exception note.
Completion was refused until reconciliation support, exception clearance, journal support, and reviewer gates were present.
Tax, legal, assurance, compilation, certification, and financial-statement signoff requests were routed outside the bookkeeping execution lane.
Duplicate matches, stale items, suspense activity, and AP or AR mismatches reopened exception rework and prevented unsupported closure.
Bookkeeping terminology was normalized to GL, AP, AR, cash, bank-feed, reconciliation, journal, and close-prep artifacts.
Missing tenant facts, approval thresholds, and retrieval hooks triggered low-confidence escalation rather than inferred coding or completion.
Conflicting public guidance, tenant policy, and reviewer instructions were escalated for precedence resolution.
Unauthorized client-record delivery was refused and routed through confidentiality and engagement-owner controls.
Write-off, payment, payroll, vendor, and policy-override approvals were rejected as management decisions and routed to authorized owners.
Tenant-specific receipt, coding, reviewer, and approval gates were recorded without weakening the public source-record baseline.
The close-prep handoff package tied trial balance, reconciliation, aging, journal, suspense, and missing-support records to reviewer gates and adjacent-owner routing.

notes: All 12 scenarios passed against the standard 0.92 threshold while preserving bookkeeping-specific workflow, source-record evidence, confidentiality, and authority boundaries.
Functionality coverage includes all shared abilities, all execution-processing-and-fulfillment abilities, and all professional-and-information-services abilities required by the contract.
